Market Intelligence Brief
Generated 16:01 ET
Dispersion trade setup emerges as index puts surge while single-name calls dominate
KEY TAKEAWAYS
  • 1. Extreme P/C divergence signals institutional dispersion hedging — index puts at 1.46 while single-names flash 0.81 call-heavy ratio.
  • 2. Negative $9.2B SPY gamma creates amplification risk into Friday's NFP, with dealer short-hedging accelerating any directional moves.
  • 3. Low 0.22 implied correlation confirms stock-picking environment favors single-name volatility over index protection.
NARRATIVE — bearish dispersion setup

The most striking development is the extreme structural divergence between index and single-name option flows, with institutional money aggressively buying index puts (1.46 ratio) while simultaneously accumulating single-name calls (0.81 ratio). This classic dispersion hedge setup gains urgency from negative $9.2B SPY gamma that will amplify any directional moves into Friday's NFP print. The 0.22 implied correlation reading confirms this is a stock-picker's market where individual names can diverge sharply from index performance. Financials emerge as the primary battleground, with massive XLF block activity spanning both calls and puts — 37,000 April 51 calls alongside 35,000 December puts creating a wide risk reversal. This coincides with technical death crosses in JPM and XLF despite GS showing golden cross momentum. The sector's mixed technical picture explains the two-way flow as institutions position for post-NFP rate volatility. Energy stands apart with XLE hitting 86.7% bullish technical scores and RSI overbought at 77, yet attracting 5,000 April 60 calls. The golden cross momentum contrasts sharply with mega-cap tech names like MSFT and META showing death cross patterns with RSI oversold readings below 30. This technical divergence reinforces the low correlation environment where sector rotation trumps broad market direction.

Layer 1: Sentiment -38Layer 2: GEX -$9.2BLayer 3: P/C split 1.46/0.81Layer 4: IC 0.22
